title: October 2021 Reading Schedule
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9wgzzLCatp8

The October reading ritual returns, with a twist: Daniel (@dungeondive) isn't reading horror exactly, but books about the devil — black magic, witchcraft, and satanic bargains across five decades of paperbacks. The slate: R.A. Lafferty's The Devil is Dead (halfway in, he cheerfully admits he has no idea what it's about, but the prose is exquisite — Lafferty being his pick for the most underrated writer in speculative fiction); Abraham Merritt's Seven Footprints to Satan, pulp adventure meets satanism; and two lurid 60s paperbacks by 'Peter Saxon' — a house pseudonym shared by multiple writers — The Curse of Rathlaw from the Guardians series and The Torturer, whose covers alone (one likely by Jeffrey Catherine Jones) justify the hunt. He confesses to secretly hoping he hates them, since the rest of the Saxon catalogue runs to $100 a paperback. The anchor is Jeff Long's 600-page The Descent, a reread prompted by his new obsession: The Empty Man, the buried 2020 film he calls the best horror movie in fifteen years, whose subterranean dread sent him straight back to Long's hollow earth. Reviews promised over the following weeks — read along if you dare.

----

Daniel hopes he'll hate a book so he won't have to pay collector prices for its sequels. Has a great first entry ever doomed your wallet to hunting an expensive out-of-print series?
